description: 25 FinOps anti-patterns organized into 5 domains. High scores indicate presence of harmful patterns.

criteria.title: Tag Sprawl & Missing Tags
criteria.description: Cloud resources lack consistent tagging or have inconsistent, redundant tag taxonomies. Cost allocation is impossible or unreliable. Untagged spend is treated as acceptable overhead rather than a governance failure.
sub_criteria: Is a significant percentage (>20%) of cloud spend untagged or unallocable to a specific team or application?
Is there no enforced tagging standard, with teams creating ad-hoc tags that differ across accounts and providers?
Is untagged spend treated as acceptable 'shared infrastructure' rather than a governance failure requiring remediation?

criteria.title: Black Box Cloud Spend
criteria.description: Cloud costs are visible only to a small group (typically finance or a single cloud admin). Engineering teams cannot see the cost of the resources they provision. Cost is discovered only during monthly invoice shock.
sub_criteria: Is cloud cost data restricted to finance or a single admin, invisible to the engineering teams who generate the spend?
Do teams discover cost overruns only through monthly invoice reviews rather than real-time visibility?
Is there no self-service cost dashboard available to engineering teams for their own services?

criteria.title: Delayed Cost Reporting
criteria.description: Cost data is stale — reports arrive days or weeks after the spend occurs. By the time anomalies are discovered, the damage is done. No near-real-time cost signal exists.
sub_criteria: Is cost reporting delayed by more than 48 hours, preventing timely response to anomalies?
Are cost reports generated manually (spreadsheets, monthly exports) rather than through automated pipelines?
Is there no automated alerting for cost anomalies, relying instead on human discovery during periodic reviews?

criteria.title: Siloed Cost Views
criteria.description: Each cloud provider, account, or team has its own cost view with no unified perspective. Total cloud economics is unknown. Finance sees invoices; engineering sees resource metrics; nobody sees both together.
sub_criteria: Does each cloud provider, account, or team have its own isolated cost view with no cross-cutting unified dashboard?
Is it impossible to answer 'What is our total cost per customer?' because data is fragmented across systems?
Do finance and engineering use different tools and numbers to discuss the same cloud spend?

criteria.title: Vanity Cost Metrics
criteria.description: The organization tracks meaningless or misleading cost metrics — total spend without context, percentage discounts without baseline, or savings numbers that cannot be verified. Metrics look good on slides but drive no real optimization.
sub_criteria: Does the organization report cost metrics that lack business context (e.g., 'we saved 20%' without baseline or verification)?
Are FinOps dashboards designed for executive presentation rather than operational decision-making?
Is there no connection between reported cost metrics and actual engineering behavior change?

criteria.title: Commitment Avoidance
criteria.description: The organization runs predominantly on on-demand pricing despite stable, predictable workloads. Fear of commitment (vendor lock-in anxiety, forecasting uncertainty) results in paying 40-70% premiums unnecessarily.
sub_criteria: Does the organization run >50% of stable workloads on on-demand pricing despite predictable usage patterns?
Is commitment avoidance driven by fear of lock-in or forecasting uncertainty rather than rational analysis?
Has the organization never calculated the on-demand premium it pays compared to commitment-based alternatives?

criteria.title: Chronic Over-Provisioning
criteria.description: Resources are systematically oversized 'just in case.' Instances run at 5-15% CPU utilization. Databases are provisioned for peak capacity that never arrives. Nobody right-sizes because nobody owns the waste.
sub_criteria: Are compute resources systematically oversized with average utilization below 20%?
Is over-provisioning justified by 'peak capacity' requirements that are never validated against actual usage data?
Are right-sizing recommendations ignored or backlogged because no team owns the responsibility to act on them?

criteria.title: Resource Hoarding & Zombie Resources
criteria.description: Teams accumulate cloud resources they no longer use — stopped instances with attached storage, orphaned snapshots, unused IP addresses, dev environments left running permanently. Cleanup is nobody's job.
sub_criteria: Are there significant numbers of stopped instances, orphaned volumes, unused snapshots, or idle load balancers accruing charges?
Do development and test environments run 24/7 despite being used only during business hours?
Is there no automated lifecycle management or scheduled cleanup of non-production resources?

criteria.title: Manual-Only Optimization
criteria.description: Cost optimization is a manual, periodic exercise — someone reviews a spreadsheet quarterly and makes recommendations. There is no automation, no continuous optimization, no integration with engineering workflows.
sub_criteria: Is cost optimization performed manually through periodic reviews rather than continuous automated processes?
Are optimization recommendations delivered as reports or tickets that sit in backlogs rather than automated actions?
Is there no integration between cost optimization tooling and engineering CI/CD or deployment workflows?

criteria.title: Discount Stacking Ignorance
criteria.description: The organization does not understand how discounts interact across providers. Savings Plans applied to already-discounted workloads, conflicting commitment types, or unused negotiated rates indicate a lack of rate optimization sophistication.
sub_criteria: Does the organization lack understanding of how discount mechanisms interact (RIs vs Savings Plans vs EDPs)?
Are commitments purchased without analysis of overlap, leaving some workloads double-covered and others uncovered?
Is there no systematic tracking of discount utilization rates across commitment types?

criteria.title: Shadow IT & Unmanaged Cloud Accounts
criteria.description: Teams or individuals create cloud accounts outside the governed organizational structure. Spend occurs on personal credit cards, department accounts, or unlinked accounts. Total cloud exposure is unknown.
sub_criteria: Do teams or individuals provision cloud resources outside the governed organizational account structure?
Is there spend on personal credit cards, departmental accounts, or unlinked cloud accounts that escapes central visibility?
Is the total number of cloud accounts and total organizational cloud exposure unknown?

criteria.title: Budget Blowout Tolerance
criteria.description: Cloud budgets are set but not enforced. Overruns are discovered after the fact with no consequences. Forecasts are fiction. The budget process is a compliance exercise disconnected from operational reality.
sub_criteria: Are cloud budget overruns routinely tolerated with no consequences or corrective action required?
Are forecasts consistently inaccurate (>20% variance) without investigation into root causes?
Is the cloud budgeting process a compliance exercise disconnected from operational decision-making?

criteria.title: FinOps Theater (Process Without Teeth)
criteria.description: The organization has FinOps titles, meetings, and dashboards but no actual optimization outcomes. FinOps is a checkbox for management reporting, not an operational discipline. Meetings happen but nothing changes.
sub_criteria: Does the organization have FinOps roles, meetings, or dashboards that produce no measurable optimization outcomes?
Are FinOps activities focused on reporting to management rather than driving engineering behavior change?
Is FinOps maturity self-assessed as 'Walk' or 'Run' without evidence of actual optimization savings?

criteria.title: Vendor Lock-In Blindness
criteria.description: The organization makes major cloud commitments (multi-year EDPs, proprietary service adoption) without evaluating exit costs, portability, or competitive alternatives. Lock-in is accepted by default rather than managed deliberately.
sub_criteria: Are major cloud commitments (EDPs, proprietary services) made without exit cost or portability analysis?
Is vendor lock-in accepted by default rather than managed as a deliberate, risk-assessed strategy?
Does the organization lack competitive cost benchmarking against alternative providers or architectures?

criteria.title: Compliance as Cost Afterthought
criteria.description: Regulatory and compliance requirements create cost surprises because they are not integrated into architecture planning. Data residency, encryption mandates, and audit requirements add unplanned spend.
sub_criteria: Do compliance requirements (data residency, encryption, logging) create unplanned cost surprises?
Is regulatory cost impact not considered during architecture design reviews?
Are compliance-driven infrastructure choices (dedicated tenancy, specific regions) made without cost impact analysis?

criteria.title: Lift-and-Shift Without Optimization
criteria.description: Workloads are migrated to the cloud with their on-premises sizing intact. Virtual machines mirror physical server specs. No cloud-native optimization occurs post-migration. The cloud becomes an expensive colocation facility.
sub_criteria: Were workloads migrated to the cloud with on-premises sizing intact, without cloud-native optimization?
Are virtual machines still sized to match physical server specifications from the data center era?
Is the cloud treated as an expensive colocation facility rather than a platform for architectural optimization?

criteria.title: Cost-Blind Architecture
criteria.description: Architecture decisions are made purely on technical merit without cost modeling. Teams choose services, instance types, and configurations based on features or familiarity, not cost-performance tradeoffs.
sub_criteria: Are architecture decisions made without cost modeling or cost-performance tradeoff analysis?
Do teams choose cloud services based on feature set or familiarity rather than cost efficiency?
Is there no cost estimation step in architecture review or deployment pipelines?

criteria.title: Scaling Without Limits
criteria.description: Autoscaling is configured without cost ceilings. A traffic spike or runaway process can scale resources to unlimited cost. There are no circuit breakers between demand signals and infrastructure provisioning.
sub_criteria: Is autoscaling configured without maximum instance limits or cost ceiling guardrails?
Has the organization experienced unexpected cost spikes from runaway scaling events?
Are there no circuit breakers between demand signals and infrastructure provisioning to prevent cost explosions?

criteria.title: Single-Cloud Tunnel Vision
criteria.description: The organization is locked into a single cloud provider without evaluating whether specific workloads would be more cost-effective elsewhere. Provider loyalty overrides economic rationality. Competitive pricing pressure is absent.
sub_criteria: Is the organization committed to a single cloud provider without periodic cost-competitiveness evaluation?
Are workload placement decisions driven by provider loyalty or organizational inertia rather than economic analysis?
Is competitive pricing pressure absent because the organization does not benchmark across providers?

criteria.title: Monolith Tax
criteria.description: Large monolithic applications prevent granular cost allocation and optimization. The entire monolith must be scaled together even if only one component is under load. Decomposition is deferred indefinitely.
sub_criteria: Do monolithic applications prevent granular cost allocation to individual features or business capabilities?
Must entire monolithic applications be scaled together even when only one component is under load?
Is decomposition into cost-optimizable components deferred indefinitely due to perceived complexity or risk?

criteria.title: Cost is IT's Problem
criteria.description: Cloud cost management is viewed as solely an IT or infrastructure responsibility. Business stakeholders who drive demand feel no accountability for cost. Engineers who provision resources never see the bill.
sub_criteria: Is cloud cost management viewed as solely an IT or infrastructure team responsibility?
Do business stakeholders who drive cloud demand feel no accountability for the resulting costs?
Do engineering teams who provision resources have no visibility into or accountability for the costs they generate?

criteria.title: Blame-Based Cost Management
criteria.description: Cost overruns trigger blame and punishment rather than systemic analysis. Teams hide cost problems instead of surfacing them. Cost discussions are adversarial between finance and engineering rather than collaborative.
sub_criteria: Do cost overruns trigger blame and punishment of individuals or teams rather than systemic analysis?
Do teams hide or defer reporting cost problems because surfacing issues leads to negative consequences?
Are cost discussions adversarial between finance and engineering rather than collaborative problem-solving?

criteria.title: FinOps Lip Service
criteria.description: Leadership talks about FinOps but does not invest in it. No dedicated team, no tooling budget, no training. FinOps is an additional duty for someone who already has a full-time job. Optimization is expected to happen organically.
sub_criteria: Does leadership endorse FinOps verbally but fail to invest in dedicated team, tooling, or training?
Is FinOps an additional duty for someone with a full-time role rather than a dedicated function?
Is cost optimization expected to happen organically without structured programs, targets, or incentives?

criteria.title: Finance-Engineering Wall
criteria.description: Finance and engineering operate as separate fiefdoms with different tools, languages, and objectives around cloud spend. Finance sees cost centers; engineering sees resource metrics. Translation between them is manual and lossy.
sub_criteria: Do finance and engineering use different tools and terminology to discuss cloud costs, creating translation overhead?
Is there no regular cross-functional forum where finance and engineering jointly review cloud economics?
Do finance team members lack technical understanding of cloud services, and engineers lack financial context?

criteria.title: Static Maturity Assumption
criteria.description: The organization treats FinOps maturity as a destination rather than a continuous journey. After initial optimization efforts, the discipline atrophies. No regular maturity assessment, no improvement targets, no benchmarking.
sub_criteria: Does the organization treat FinOps as a completed project rather than a continuous improvement discipline?
Has initial optimization momentum stalled with no ongoing maturity assessment or improvement targets?
Is there no external benchmarking or peer comparison to drive continued advancement?
